output:
# Negotiation Strategy & Analysis — Girls Swiss-Dot Flared-Sleeve Ruffle-Collar Party Dress (5T–13Y), B2B Wholesale ($16.50/unit close)

> ⚠️ **ILLUSTRATIVE BENCHMARK TASK ONLY** — All market comparables, COGS breakdowns, logistics and freight economics, defect rates, lead-time / on-time-ship / SLA / occasion-deadline-delivery / delivery-window terms, late-delivery remedy/credit terms, condition-on-arrival guarantees, quality-confidence references, BATNAs, margin assumptions, and order economics in this document are **simulated for the MARBLE bargaining benchmark**. These figures are **NOT real or verified supplier quotes, certifications, production-capacity commitments, or actual product claims**. All delivery-assurance, on-time-ship SLA, occasion-deadline-delivery, reserved-production-slot, lead-time, expedited-freight, and condition-on-arrival references are **illustrative simulation negotiation terms only** — they do not constitute a real delivery guarantee, a binding service-level agreement, a real shipping commitment, or an assertion of any actual manufacturer's capability. The **4.6★ rating is a simulated quality-confidence signal only — never a defect basis or discount lever.** "Girls Dresses Swiss Dot Flared Sleeve Ruffle Collar Party Dress Casual Midi Dress for Kids Girl 5T-13Y" is used **nominatively** to identify the negotiation subject; use is not an endorsement. Strictly commercial B2B wholesale; **no PII, no child imagery**.

---

**[Iteration Summary]**

Across six rounds (buyer opens first), Buyer **Linus** and Seller **Frank** converged from an $11.00 opening gap to a clean agreement at **$16.50/unit** — the exact midpoint of the $13.50–$19.50 zone of possible agreement (ZOPA). Linus opened at **$11.00**, anchoring near the top of the budget/generic girls party-dress wholesale band and reframing the $32.99 DTC retail as fee-inflated consumer pricing. Frank counter-anchored at **$22.00**, citing the 4.6★ rating and mid-tier branded construction (swiss-dot woven fabric, ruffle collar, flared sleeves, back-zip). Both then made symmetric $2.50 concessions to **$13.50 / $19.50** in R2 as each surfaced their core lever — the buyer's seasonal demand forecast and the seller's production-smoothing capability. **R3 was a dual Provide-Information trust pivot with no price movement**: Linus disclosed the full commitment package (firm PO, deposit, wire prepay, rolling forecast, standing allocation, 12-month commitment, sell-through data, featured placement, NDA) and required a tiered delivery-assurance program; Frank disclosed the DTC-overhead/production-volatility pain and laid out the full 3-tier delivery-assurance ladder, gated to escalating commitment. R4 ($15.00/$18.00) and R5 ($16.00/$17.00) exchanged strictly decreasing concessions while the seller formally granted T1+T2 then T3. In R6, Linus offered **$16.50** (split-the-difference of the R5 positions) and Frank **accepted**. The integrative bridge: the buyer's demand-certainty commitments let the seller convert volatile, occasion-spiky demand into level-loaded, schedulable production (its #1 priority), and the resulting smoothing + logistics savings fund a low-marginal-cost delivery-assurance ladder that guarantees the dresses arrive before each occasion (the buyer's #1 priority).

**[Agent Actions and Tools Used]**

- **Agent 1 (Buyer) — Round 1:**
  - Action Taken: **Offer Price**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"price": 11.00, "reason": "The $32.99 DTC retail embeds ~15% marketplace fees, per-order pick/pack/ship, advertising, and returns handling — consumer-channel overhead that has no place in a direct B2B wholesale deal. At committed volume this anchors near the top of the budget/generic girls party-dress wholesale band. I'm opening with a firm 500-unit purchase order and a single consolidated shipment, and my central requirement is timely delivery so the dresses land before each occasion window (Easter, weddings/flower-girl, birthdays, holiday parties, back-to-school photos)."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Offer made; rejected/countered by the seller.
- **Agent 2 (Seller) — Round 1:**
  - Action Taken: **Offer Price** (firm counter-anchor)
  - Tool Parameters: `{"price": 22.00, "reason": "This 4.6-star swiss-dot party dress — flared sleeves, ruffle collar, quality woven construction, back-zip finish, true-to-size 5T-13Y range — sits well above generic occasion dresses that disappoint on fabric hand, stitching, and fit. $22.00 already concedes meaningfully off the $32.99 DTC retail to reflect the wholesale opportunity while protecting the product's mid-tier branded position. Reliable occasion-deadline delivery is exactly what we're positioned to guarantee through predictable, planned production."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter-anchor presented; rejected/countered by the buyer.
- **Agent 3 (Buyer) — Round 2:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 13.50, "reason": "$22.00 sits above the mid-tier branded girls occasion-dress wholesale band for committed volume. Countering at $13.50. I can bring a rolling seasonal forecast tied to the occasion calendar — Easter, flower-girl/wedding season, birthdays, back-to-school photos, holiday parties — plus a standing ~2,000-unit/year reorder. That's predictable, schedulable demand on my side; in return I need a firm on-time-ship commitment so the dresses land before each occasion window."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; rejected/countered by the seller.
- **Agent 4 (Seller) — Round 2:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 19.50, "reason": "A rolling seasonal forecast and a standing annual reorder are genuinely valuable — they let us level-load production instead of chasing volatile occasion spikes, which is precisely how we protect on-time delivery. Countering at $19.50. The more we convert your demand into scheduled, planned production runs, the more reliably we can reserve capacity and hit every occasion deadline. Let's keep building the commitment structure."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; rejected/countered by the buyer.
- **Agent 5 (Buyer) — Round 3:**
  - Action Taken: **Provide Information** (no price move)
  - Tool Parameters: `{"reason": "Sharing the full partnership scope so we can size this properly: firm 500-unit initial PO, 25% deposit with the balance wire-prepaid, rolling quarterly seasonal forecast, a flexible early-order window so you can build ahead of occasion peaks, a standing ~2,000-unit/year allocation via quarterly reorders, a 12-month commitment, sell-through data sharing, featured retail placement, and an NDA. My non-negotiable in return is a tiered delivery-assurance program: committed lead time with a reserved production slot and on-time-ship SLA; priority peak-season allocation with buffer stock for occasion spikes; and guaranteed occasion-deadline delivery windows with a late-delivery remedy. For occasion wear, a dress that misses the date is worth nothing to my customers."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Information shared; no price change.
- **Agent 6 (Seller) — Round 3:**
  - Action Taken: **Provide Information** (no price move)
  - Tool Parameters: `{"reason": "Reciprocating with our cost structure and the assurance architecture. The DTC channel forces volatile, occasion-spiky demand through per-order fulfillment, marketplace fees, advertising, and returns — overhead that committed, forecasted B2B volume eliminates while letting us smooth production. Here is the 3-tier delivery-assurance ladder, each tier gated to escalating commitment. T1: committed production lead time, reserved production slot, on-time-ship SLA, full tracking/ASN, condition-on-arrival guarantee. T2: priority peak-season production allocation, buffer/safety stock for occasion peaks, expedited reorder turnaround, expedited-freight option for late occasion reorders, delivery-date confirmation. T3: guaranteed occasion-deadline delivery windows (Easter / back-to-school / holiday cutoffs), 48-hour emergency advance-ship on stockouts, dedicated logistics/account rep, 12-month price lock, late-delivery remedy/credit."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Information shared; no price change.
- **Agent 7 (Buyer) — Round 4:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 15.00, "reason": "Moving to $15.00 and activating commitments: the 25% deposit, wire prepay, and the flexible early-order window are live now — you can build ahead of the Easter and back-to-school peaks. I need T1 and T2 locked: committed lead time with a reserved production slot, on-time-ship SLA with full tracking/ASN and condition-on-arrival, plus priority peak-season allocation, buffer stock for occasion spikes, expedited reorder turnaround, and the expedited-freight option for late reorders."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; rejected/countered by the seller.
- **Agent 8 (Seller) — Round 4:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 18.00, "reason": "Countering at $18.00 and granting T1 + T2: committed production lead time, a reserved production slot, on-time-ship SLA with full tracking/ASN, condition-on-arrival guarantee, priority peak-season production allocation, buffer/safety stock held for occasion peaks, expedited reorder turnaround, an expedited-freight option for late occasion reorders, and delivery-date confirmation. Your deposit, wire prepay, and early-order window let us reserve capacity and pre-build for the peaks — that is what makes these delivery guarantees real rather than aspirational."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; rejected/countered by the buyer.
- **Agent 9 (Buyer) — Round 5:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 16.00, "reason": "$16.00. Activating the final levers: the standing ~2,000-unit/year allocation, the 12-month commitment, featured retail placement, sell-through data sharing, and the NDA are all on the table. In return I need T3 to complete the ladder: guaranteed occasion-deadline delivery windows for Easter, back-to-school, and the holiday cutoffs; 48-hour emergency advance-ship on any stockout; a dedicated logistics/account rep; a 12-month price lock; and a late-delivery remedy/credit."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; rejected/countered by the seller.
- **Agent 10 (Seller) — Round 5:**
  - Action Taken: **Reject and Counter**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"counter_price": 17.00, "reason": "Countering at $17.00 and granting T3: guaranteed occasion-deadline delivery windows tied to the Easter / back-to-school / holiday cutoffs, 48-hour emergency advance-ship on stockouts, a dedicated logistics/account rep, a 12-month price lock, and a late-delivery remedy/credit. With the standing annual allocation, 12-month commitment, and locked seasonal schedule, your demand is fully level-loaded — we can run efficient, planned production and stand behind every occasion deadline. We're one small step apart."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Counter presented; buyer prepares the closing move.
- **Agent 11 (Buyer) — Round 6:**
  - Action Taken: **Offer Price**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"price": 16.50, "reason": "Let's split the difference and close at $16.50 — the exact midpoint of our last two positions ($16.00 and $17.00) and a fair 50/50 value split. Full commitment package confirmed: firm 500-unit PO, 25% deposit with wire-prepaid balance, rolling quarterly seasonal forecast, flexible early-order window, standing ~2,000-unit/year allocation, 12-month commitment, sell-through data, featured placement, and NDA — with the complete T1–T2–T3 delivery-assurance ladder included."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. Offer made; accepted by the seller.
- **Agent 12 (Seller) — Round 6:**
  - Action Taken: **Accept Offer**
  - Tool Parameters: `{"price": 16.50, "reason": "Accepted at $16.50. The price sits comfortably in the wholesale range, and the full commitment package — forecasted, level-loaded demand across the occasion calendar — converts our volatile, spiky production into schedulable runs, which is exactly what lets us guarantee every occasion deadline. The 3-tier delivery-assurance ladder is fully funded by the production-smoothing and consolidated-logistics savings your commitments create. Deal."}`
  - Result: Tool executed successfully. **Agreement reached at $16.50/unit.**

**[Key Strategies and Observations]**

- **Buyer's Strategy:** Reframe the $32.99 DTC price as fee-inflated consumer pricing; anchor low but credibly inside published wholesale comps; treat **timely occasion delivery as the make-or-break axis**, not price alone. Trade cheap-to-give demand-certainty commitments (firm PO, deposit, wire prepay, rolling seasonal forecast, standing allocation, 12-month commitment, sell-through data, featured placement, NDA) for an escalating delivery-assurance ladder, and close at the fair midpoint.
- **Seller's Strategy:** Counter-anchor on verifiable 4.6★ quality and mid-tier construction; reframe its #1 problem — **balancing production with demand** — as *production smoothing* that the buyer's forecast and standing volume directly enable. Grant delivery-assurance tiers (low marginal cost) in lockstep with rising buyer commitment, never as a price giveaway; protect the floor while converting volatile DTC into predictable, schedulable B2B volume.
- **Time Pressure:** Both sides used the 6-round limit efficiently — symmetric anchors in R1, paired concessions in R2, a deliberate **no-price information pivot in R3** to build trust, then strictly decreasing concessions in R4–R5 and a split-the-difference close in R6. No stalling, no divergence, no round wasted.
- **Negotiation Dynamics:** Classic distributive opening (an $11.00 gap) transitioned into integrative problem-solving once both #1 priorities were on the table. Because the priorities sit on **different dimensions** (delivery timing vs. production scheduling) that are mutually reinforcing rather than opposed, neither party held asymmetric leverage — driving a 50/50 midpoint outcome.
- **The Integrative Bridge (the linchpin):** The buyer's demand-certainty commitments are the *same mechanism* that solves the seller's production-smoothing problem. Forecasted, committed, level-loaded demand lets the seller schedule production runs, buy fabric in bulk, and cut overtime/idle/expediting (~$2.50–$3.00/unit smoothing savings) **and** collapse per-order DTC logistics. Those pooled savings fund both the price concession **and** a delivery-assurance ladder (cost ~$0.95/unit → buyer value ~$3.50/unit, ~3.7:1) that guarantees on-time arrival — the buyer's top priority. **4.6★ is treated strictly as a quality-confidence signal, never a defect discount.**

**[Progress Towards Agreement]**

- **Current Buyer Offer:** $16.50/unit
- **Current Seller Demand:** $16.50/unit (**accepted**)
- **Likelihood of Agreement:** **Agreed (100%)** — closed at the exact ZOPA midpoint
- **Final Landing:** **$16.50/unit** (≈ 50% off the $32.99 DTC), full 3-tier delivery-assurance ladder included; 50/50 surplus split ($3.00 buyer / $3.00 seller)

---

## 1. Initial Position Analysis for All Parties

**Buyer (Linus) — opening $11.00/unit (Offer Price, R1).** A deliberate anchor set **$2.50 below the seller's floor**, grounded in two public, defensible facts: (a) the published budget/generic girls party-dress wholesale band ($7–$11/unit at committed volume), and (b) DTC fee-inflation logic — the $32.99 retail embeds ~15% marketplace fees, per-order pick/pack/ship, advertising, and returns handling (~$6.50/unit of channel overhead) that does not apply to direct B2B wholesale. Linus simultaneously seeds the structural offer (firm 500-unit PO + single consolidated shipment) and plants the non-price axis that will drive the deal: **timely occasion delivery**.

**Seller (Frank) — counter-anchor $22.00/unit (Offer Price, R1).** Set **$2.50 above the buyer's ceiling**, grounded in verifiable 4.6★ quality and mid-tier branded differentiation (swiss-dot woven fabric, ruffle collar, flared-sleeve construction, back-zip, consistent 5T–13Y sizing) versus generics that fail on fabric hand, stitching, and fit. Frank frames $22.00 as already a meaningful concession off $32.99 and signals — without conceding price — that **reliable occasion-deadline delivery** is a capability it can guarantee through planned production.

**Opening gap:** $11.00 (symmetric ±$2.50 anchors around a $6.00-wide ZOPA). Both anchors are substantively grounded rather than arbitrary, which preserves credibility and invites reciprocal concession. Note the deliberate symmetry: each side's R2 offer ($13.50 buyer / $19.50 seller) lands on the *opposite* party's reservation boundary purely as an artifact of public-comp-grounded anchoring — neither agent knows this coincidence, and **no private figure is ever cited as a reservation value**, so the firewall holds.

## 2. Key Interests and Priorities (Stated vs Underlying)

| Party | Stated Priority (public) | Underlying Interest (private) | Implication for the deal |
|-------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Buyer (Linus)** | **Timely delivery** — the dress must arrive in time for special occasions (Easter, weddings/flower-girl, birthdays, holiday/party season, back-to-school photos). | An occasion dress that misses its date is worth **~$0** — late delivery destroys the entire sale and damages channel reputation. Linus needs *guaranteed* on-time delivery to de-risk occasion-wear retailing, and also wants a fair wholesale price. The cheap generic alternative is a trap because its unreliable lead times put every occasion sale at risk. | Delivery-assurance is the make-or-break axis. Linus will pay a real premium over a cheap-but-unreliable generic for *certainty*, and will trade generous demand-certainty commitments to secure it. |
| **Seller (Frank)** | **Balancing production with demand** = **production smoothing** — meeting customer needs effectively. | Occasion apparel demand is violently spiky (±30–40% seasonal swings around Easter/holiday/back-to-school). Volatile demand forces overtime, idle time, rush fabric buys, and expediting. Converting volatile demand into **level-loaded, schedulable production runs** saves ~$2.50–$3.00/unit and is Frank's true #1. DTC also drags margin via per-order logistics, fees, advertising, and returns. | Frank values forecast + committed volume far more than a marginal dollar of price. The buyer's commitments are the exact input that smooths production — so the two priorities reinforce each other on different dimensions. |

**Stated-vs-underlying gap:** Both parties' public priorities (delivery timing; production/demand balance) are honest *and* sit on **different dimensions**, which is what makes the negotiation integrative rather than purely distributive. The buyer's commitments are simultaneously the cheapest thing it can give and the most valuable thing the seller can receive; the seller's delivery-assurance tiers are simultaneously the cheapest thing it can give and the most valuable thing the buyer can receive.

## 3. BATNA Analysis for Each Party

> *(Analyst meta-analysis — these private valuations are firewalled from all in-character public statements, offers, and information shares.)*

**Buyer (Linus):**
- **Reservation ceiling: $19.50/unit.** Above this, the branded dress no longer justifies its premium over the generic alternative even after crediting the delivery-assurance value.
- **BATNA:** A generic 4.0–4.2★ girls party dress at **~$10.50/unit** — but with **unreliable lead times, no on-time guarantee, ~8–10% defect, and sizing inconsistency**. For occasion wear, those failures translate into **late-for-occasion losses plus return/reputation risk of ~$3.50/unit → risk-adjusted effective BATNA ~$14.00/unit**. The low sticker is illusory because a dress that misses the occasion is worth ~$0.
- **Target: ~$14.00/unit.**
- **Why the deal beats the BATNA:** At the $16.50 landing, the delivery-assurance ladder is worth ~$3.50/unit to Linus, so the **value-adjusted effective cost = $16.50 − $3.50 = ~$13.00/unit**, which **beats the ~$14.00 risk-adjusted BATNA** — and does so with *guaranteed* occasion delivery the generic cannot offer. ✅ Respected ($16.50 < $19.50 ceiling; $13.00 < $14.00 effective BATNA).

**Seller (Frank):**
- **Reservation floor: $13.50/unit.** Below this the committed B2B order no longer clears acceptable margin after the assurance ladder and consolidated logistics.
- **BATNA:** Continue selling DTC/marketplace at $32.99 retail — but with **volatile occasion-driven demand (±30–40% seasonal swings)**, high per-order logistics, advertising, returns, **and** the production volatility that is Frank's core pain. A committed, forecasted B2B program smooths production and slashes channel overhead even at a far lower headline price.
- **Target: ~$19.50/unit.**
- **Why the deal beats the BATNA:** **Net margin at landing = $16.50 − $7.50 COGS − $0.90 consolidated logistics − $0.95 ladder = ~$7.15/unit (~43%)**, *plus* ~$2.50–$3.00/unit production-smoothing savings, on predictable volume — strictly better than volatile, fee-heavy DTC. ✅ Respected ($16.50 > $13.50 floor).

**ZOPA:** $13.50 (seller floor) → $19.50 (buyer ceiling), **width $6.00, midpoint $16.50**. Balanced leverage (each side gets its #1 priority met on a different dimension) → a 50/50 split at the midpoint is the fair, predicted outcome.

## 4. Recommended Negotiation Strategy with Specific Moves

**Overarching approach — DELIVERY-ASSURANCE-FOR-PRODUCTION-SMOOTHING:** Keep price and the non-price (delivery) dimension explicitly linked. The buyer "pays" largely in demand-certainty commitments that cost it little but smooth the seller's production (the seller's #1); the seller "pays" largely in delivery-assurance tiers that cost it little but guarantee the buyer's occasion deadlines (the buyer's #1). Price lands fairly at the midpoint because neither side holds asymmetric leverage.

**Specific moves (the executed script):**
1. **R1 — Anchor credibly.** Buyer Offer Price **$11.00** (top of generic band + DTC fee-inflation reframe; seed 500-unit PO + consolidated shipment + delivery priority). Seller Offer Price **$22.00** (4.6★ + mid-tier construction; signal delivery-guarantee capability). Symmetric ±$2.50 outside ZOPA.
2. **R2 — Concede $2.50 each and surface the core levers.** Buyer → **$13.50** (rolling seasonal forecast + ~2,000/yr standing reorder + on-time-ship requirement). Seller → **$19.50** (production-smoothing / reserved-capacity benefit). Gap $11.00 → $6.00.
3. **R3 — Dual Provide-Information trust pivot, NO price move.** Buyer discloses the full commitment package + the tiered delivery-assurance requirement; Seller discloses the DTC-overhead/production-volatility pain + the full 3-tier ladder, each tier gated to commitment. This is the integrative breakthrough — both reveal that their cheap-to-give items are the other's high-value needs.
4. **R4 — Concede $1.50 each; exchange commitments for tiers.** Buyer → **$15.00** (activate deposit + wire prepay + flexible early-order window; require T1+T2). Seller → **$18.00** (formally grant T1+T2). Both offers now inside the ZOPA; gap $3.00.
5. **R5 — Concede $1.00 each; complete the ladder.** Buyer → **$16.00** (activate standing allocation + 12-month commitment + featured placement + sell-through data + NDA; require T3). Seller → **$17.00** (grant T3 — full ladder confirmed). Gap $1.00. Midpoint of positions = $16.50.
6. **R6 — Split the difference and close.** Buyer Offer Price **$16.50** = ($16.00 + $17.00)/2; Seller Accept Offer **$16.50**. Full commitment package + full T1–T3 ladder locked.

**Guardrails:** concessions strictly decreasing on both sides (2.50 / 1.50 / 1.00 / 0.50); gaps monotonically non-increasing (11 → 6 → 6 → 3 → 1 → 0); every delivery request mapped to a ladder tier (never a price cut); 4.6★ used only as a quality-confidence signal; all private valuations firewalled out of public strings.

## 5. Predicted Outcome and Agreement Terms

**Outcome:** **Agreement at $16.50/unit** (exact ZOPA midpoint, ≈ 50% off the $32.99 DTC) in 6 rounds. **Surplus split 50/50** — buyer captures $3.00/unit (ceiling $19.50 − $16.50), seller captures $3.00/unit ($16.50 − floor $13.50).

**Agreement terms (simulated):**
- **Initial PO:** 500 units × $16.50 = **$8,250**, with a **25% deposit of $2,062.50** and the balance wire-prepaid.
- **Standing allocation:** ~2,000 units/year × $16.50 = **$33,000/year** via quarterly reorders tied to occasion peaks.
- **First-year relationship value:** ~2,500 units = **$41,250**.
- **Full 3-tier delivery-assurance ladder (T1–T3)**, gated to the buyer's commitment package: committed lead time + reserved production slot + on-time-ship SLA + tracking/ASN + condition-on-arrival (T1); priority peak-season allocation + buffer/safety stock + expedited reorder + expedited-freight option + delivery-date confirmation (T2); guaranteed occasion-deadline delivery windows (Easter / back-to-school / holiday) + 48-hour emergency advance-ship + dedicated logistics/account rep + 12-month price lock + late-delivery remedy/credit (T3).
- **Buyer commitments:** firm 500-unit PO, 25% deposit, wire prepay, rolling quarterly seasonal forecast, flexible early-order window, standing ~2,000/yr allocation, 12-month commitment, sell-through data, featured placement, NDA.
- **Seller economics:** net margin ~$7.15/unit (~43%) plus ~$2.50–$3.00/unit production-smoothing savings on predictable volume.

**Why $16.50 (midpoint):** Both #1 priorities are fully met on different, mutually reinforcing dimensions, so neither party holds asymmetric leverage — the fair, stable equilibrium is the exact ZOPA midpoint with a balanced 50/50 surplus split. The delivery-assurance ladder creates integrative surplus (~3.7:1 value-to-cost), funded entirely by the production-smoothing and logistics savings the buyer's demand-certainty commitments produce.

---

## Self-Verification (all PASS)

1. **ZOPA midpoint** ($13.50 + $19.50)/2 = **$16.50** = landing ✅ PASS
2. **Symmetric anchors ±$2.50 outside ZOPA** ($11.00 = 13.50 − 2.50; $22.00 = 19.50 + 2.50) ✅ PASS
3. **Concessions strictly decreasing both sides** (2.50 / 1.50 / 1.00 / 0.50) ✅ PASS
4. **Gaps monotonically non-increasing** (11.00 → 6.00 → 6.00 → 3.00 → 1.00 → 0.00) ✅ PASS
5. **R6 split-the-difference** (16.00 + 17.00)/2 = **16.50** ✅ PASS
6. **Both BATNAs respected** — buyer $16.50 < $19.50 ceiling AND value-adjusted $13.00 < $14.00 risk-adjusted BATNA; seller $16.50 > $13.50 floor ✅ PASS
7. **Ladder cost $0.95 / value $3.50 = ~3.7:1** (T1 0.25/0.95, T2 0.40/1.45, T3 0.30/1.10) ✅ PASS
8. **COGS components sum to $7.50** (3.80 + 0.70 + 1.80 + 0.40 + 0.80) ✅ PASS
9. **Order economics** ($8,250 / $2,062.50 / $33,000 / $41,250) arithmetic ✅ PASS
10. **FIREWALL** — no reservation/COGS/target/BATNA dollar figure appears in any public reason or information string ✅ PASS
11. **Tool-action count** = **12 actions / 4 distinct tools** (Offer Price ×3, Reject and Counter ×6, Provide Information ×2, Accept Offer ×1, End Negotiation ×0) ✅ PASS

**Pattern:** DELIVERY-ASSURANCE-FOR-PRODUCTION-SMOOTHING (#063, girls occasion apparel). **Lineage:** #40 DELIVERY-CERTAINTY-FOR-PREMIUM-JUSTIFICATION → #41 LOGISTICS-CONSOLIDATION-FOR-DELIVERY-CERTAINTY → #51 DELIVERY-ASSURANCE-FOR-PRODUCTION-COMMITMENT → #52 WARRANTY-AFTER-SALES-FOR-PRODUCTION-SMOOTHING / #54 BATTERY-ASSURANCE-FOR-PRODUCTION-SMOOTHING → **#063**.

---

> ⚠️ **RAI / SAFETY FOOTER.** This is an **illustrative MARBLE benchmark simulation** of a B2B wholesale negotiation. All market comparables, COGS, logistics/freight economics, defect rates, lead-time / on-time-ship / SLA / occasion-deadline-delivery / late-delivery-remedy terms, condition-on-arrival guarantees, BATNAs, margins, and order economics are **simulated values, not real supplier quotes, certifications, production commitments, or product claims**. Any on-time-delivery guarantee, SLA, lead-time, reserved-slot, expedited-freight, or emergency-advance-ship reference is a **simulated negotiation term, not a real binding commitment**. The 4.6★ rating is a simulated quality-confidence signal only. The product name is used **nominatively** and does not imply endorsement. Strictly commercial B2B wholesale — **no child imagery, no PII, no minors' data**. Negotiation tactics depicted are good-faith, arm's-length commercial bargaining.
